title: Compliance Drift Detection (Was Compliant, Now Not)

compliance.assurance_rationale: Detects unauthorized configuration changes by identifying devices that drift from compliant to non-compliant state.
compliance.controlObjective: Identifies when a device moves from COMPLIANT to NON_COMPLIANT for a given policy type so unauthorised or untracked change (CM-3) can be investigated.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Alert or report `UC-5.13.32` listing deviceName, complianceType, and status transition; include Splunk time range in the case record for the security or change team.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Identifies configuration changes outside of authorized change management processes by detecting compliance drift.
compliance.controlObjective: Gives a detective signal that configuration on financial-reporting–relevant network equipment may have changed outside approved change management (ITGC change controls).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Same drift report; tie each row to a change ticket ID via lookup or manual reconciliation for external audit (SOX ITGC workpapers).

dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:compliance` from the `compliance` modular input in `TA_cisco_catalyst` (Splunkbase 7538). Same data feed as UC-5.13.28. This UC detects *state transitions* — devices whose `complianceStatus` changed from COMPLIANT to NON_COMPLIANT between consecutive polls, indicating configuration drift occurred between the two check cycles.
spl:
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:compliance"
| sort deviceName, complianceType, _time
| streamstats current=f last(complianceStatus) as prev_status by deviceName, complianceType
| where complianceStatus="NON_COMPLIANT" AND prev_status="COMPLIANT"
| table _time, deviceName, complianceType, prev_status, complianceStatus
| sort _time
description: Detects the exact moment a device drifts from COMPLIANT to NON_COMPLIANT, identifying configuration changes that occurred between compliance poll cycles — the strongest signal of unauthorised modification, failed change procedures, or accidental rollback that UC-5.13.28's point-in-time snapshot cannot provide.
value: UC-5.13.28 tells you how many devices are non-compliant *now*. This UC tells you which devices *just became* non-compliant — meaning someone or something changed the configuration since the last check. For NIST CM-3 (Configuration Change Control), this is the detective control that catches unauthorised changes. For SOX ITGC, a compliance drift event on a financially-relevant device without a corresponding change ticket is a potential ITGC finding. The drift detection is more operationally actionable than a static non-compliance count because it identifies *new* problems rather than chronic known exceptions.
implementation: Same `compliance` input as UC-5.13.28. Uses `streamstats` to compare consecutive polls per device per compliance type. Schedule as alert: every 1 hour (matching the compliance poll interval). Route drift events to the change management team.
visualization: (1) Table: _time, deviceName, complianceType, prev_status → complianceStatus — showing the transition timestamp. (2) Single value: count of drift events in the last 24h (red ≥ 1). (3) Timeline panel showing drift events as markers on a time axis. (4) Correlation view: drilldown to `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs"` filtered to the same deviceName and time window to find the change that caused the drift.

references.title: Catalyst Center Intent API — Compliance endpoint
NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — CM-3 Configuration Change Control

knownFalsePositives:
**Golden template update causing mass drift detection.** When the golden template is changed, all devices are re-evaluated. Devices compliant under the old template may appear to 'drift' to NON_COMPLIANT under the new one. Distinguish by correlating with `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs"` for template changes in the same window. Suppress by requiring drift events to persist beyond a 24-hour grace period after template updates.

**Planned configuration push triggering temporary drift.** During a staged configuration deployment, devices transitionally show NON_COMPLIANT between receiving the push and the next compliance check. Distinguish by checking ITSM change records for approved changes on the drifted devices. Suppress by correlating with change tickets: `| lookup change_records deviceName, _time OUTPUT change_id | where isnull(change_id)` to flag only *unapproved* drift.

**Compliance status oscillation due to API timing.** If the compliance check and the TA poll don't align precisely, a device may appear to flip between COMPLIANT and NON_COMPLIANT across consecutive polls. Distinguish by checking whether the drift reverses on the next poll. Suppress by requiring the NON_COMPLIANT state to persist for 2+ consecutive polls.

**EOX compliance type triggering drift on lifecycle milestone dates.** When a device reaches its End-of-Life date, EOX compliance flips from COMPLIANT to NON_COMPLIANT — this is a lifecycle event, not a configuration change. Distinguish by checking `complianceType=EOX`. Suppress by filtering EOX drift events to a separate asset management dashboard.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• UC-5.13.28 (Compliance Status Overview) must be operational — same `compliance` data feed.
• The compliance input must poll frequently enough (default 3600s / 1 hour) to detect drift in a timely manner. The precision of drift detection is bounded by the poll interval — changes between polls are detected on the next cycle.
• For SOX ITGC compliance, identify which devices are financially-relevant. Maintain a `sox_in_scope_devices` lookup for filtering.
• For CM-3 correlation, have access to `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs"` (UC-5.13.46) and/or ITSM change records for change-vs-drift reconciliation.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Same `compliance` input as UC-5.13.28. No additional configuration.

For drift detection accuracy, ensure the compliance input runs consistently (no gaps). Gaps in polling create false drift events when the input resumes.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:compliance"
| sort deviceName, complianceType, _time
| streamstats current=f last(complianceStatus) as prev_status by deviceName, complianceType
| where complianceStatus="NON_COMPLIANT" AND prev_status="COMPLIANT"
| table _time, deviceName, complianceType, prev_status, complianceStatus
| sort _time
```

Why `streamstats current=f last(complianceStatus) as prev_status`: for each event, this looks at the *previous* event for the same device + compliance type pair and captures its status. When `prev_status=COMPLIANT` and `complianceStatus=NON_COMPLIANT`, a drift occurred between those two polls.

Why `sort deviceName, complianceType, _time` before `streamstats`: ensures events are in chronological order per device per type. Without this sort, `streamstats` may compare non-adjacent events and produce false detections.

Why this is a **run-tier** UC: `streamstats` is computationally expensive because it processes events sequentially. For a large fleet over a long time range, the search can take minutes. It's appropriate for scheduled analysis, not real-time dashboards.

For change-record correlation (CM-3 / SOX ITGC):
```spl
<base drift search above>
| lookup change_records deviceName, start_time, end_time OUTPUT change_id, change_status
| eval drift_type=if(isnotnull(change_id), "Approved change", "UNAPPROVED — investigate")
| table _time, deviceName, complianceType, drift_type, change_id
```

Schedule as Alert: cron `0 * * * *` (hourly, matching the compliance poll cadence), time range `-2h to now`, trigger when `drift_type="UNAPPROVED — investigate"`. Route to the change management / security team.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Intentional test: in a lab, make a manual CLI change on a compliant device (e.g., add an unauthorised ACL entry). Wait for the next two compliance polls (2 hours). The drift search should show that device transitioning from COMPLIANT to NON_COMPLIANT.

(b) Run over the last 7 days. Each drift event should correspond to either a known change (approved template push) or an investigation-worthy event (unauthorised modification).

(c) Cross-reference with `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs"` for the drifted devices — was there a configuration change logged by Catalyst Center in the same window?

(d) For SOX environments: verify that SOX-scoped devices are correctly identified by the `sox_in_scope_devices` lookup and that unapproved drift events generate the expected alerts.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Alerting:
• Change Management team: receives all drift events.
• Security Operations: receives only *unapproved* drift events (those without a matching change record).
• SOX/Audit team: receives drift events for financially-relevant devices.

Runbook (owner: Change Management):
1. Receive drift alert. Note `deviceName`, `complianceType`, `_time`.
2. Check ITSM for an approved change record covering that device and time.
3. If approved change exists: annotate the drift event as 'expected.' No further action.
4. If NO approved change: this is an unauthorised configuration modification.
   - Check `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs" deviceName="<device>" earliest=-4h` for who made the change.
   - If a user is identified: investigate whether it was intentional (and needs a retroactive change record) or accidental.
   - If no user is identified: the change may have been made via direct CLI access bypassing Catalyst Center. Escalate to security operations.
5. Remediate: push the golden template from Catalyst Center to restore compliance.
6. Document the event and root cause in the compliance evidence folder.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Many false drift events after TA restart** — when the TA restarts, `streamstats` may compare the first post-restart event with the last pre-restart event, creating false transitions. Filter by requiring `_time - prev_time < 7200` (events within 2 hours of each other).

• **Mass drift after golden template update** — expected. Annotate with the template change date. These are 'expected drift,' not unauthorised changes.

• **`streamstats` search is very slow** — processing millions of compliance events sequentially is expensive. Narrow the time range to `-24h` for the alert, or pre-aggregate to `| stats latest(complianceStatus) as status by deviceName, complianceType, _time` before `streamstats`.

• **Drift events for EOX compliance type** — EOX status changes are lifecycle events, not configuration changes. Filter with `| where complianceType != "EOX"` for configuration-drift-only analysis.

• **No drift events ever detected** — either no devices have changed compliance status (good!), or the compliance input isn't running long enough to have consecutive polls for comparison. Verify with `| stats count by deviceName, complianceType | where count >= 2`.

• **Same device shows drift every poll cycle** — the device is oscillating between COMPLIANT and NON_COMPLIANT. Investigate the root cause: likely an unstable golden template or a device that auto-reverts its configuration.

• **Change-record correlation misses matches** — ensure the `change_records` lookup time windows align with the drift event timestamps. Consider a 2-hour buffer on each side.

• **`prev_status` is null for the first event per device** — expected. The first event has no predecessor. The `streamstats current=f` flag skips the current event's own status, and `last()` returns null when there's no previous event. The `where prev_status="COMPLIANT"` filter naturally excludes these.
grandmaExplanation: We catch the exact moment a network device's settings change from 'approved' to 'unapproved.' If the change was planned, we match it to the change ticket. If it was not planned, we flag it as a potential unauthorised modification that needs investigation — this is what auditors care about most.
